name: The Tingkat Nobody Ordered
alias: the midnight delivery · the knock that wears a voice you know, for a house that ordered nothing
body: It is the knock that wakes you, three soft taps low on the door, down near where a child's hand would reach, and then the voice, and the voice is the trouble, because it belongs to someone in the house: your mother calling you by your milk name, the one only family uses; your husband saying he forgot his keys again, sheepish, exactly as he always is; a child you put to bed an hour ago asking, sleepily, to be let in. You lie in the dark doing the arithmetic of who is home, listening for the breathing beside you, for the small sounds of the flat, the fridge, the corridor fan, the neighbour's aircon condenser dripping onto the ledge. The arithmetic comes out wrong. On the floor of the corridor, when you finally rise and look through the gap above the gate, sits a tingkat, a stacked tiffin carrier, lid clasped, faintly warm, with a delivery slip tucked under the handle for a household that ordered nothing, on a night when no rider would climb to this floor.

The slip has your unit number and no shop, no app logo, no order code, only your block and your floor in a hand that is not printed. The rule passed down is about the threshold: the carrier is left outside, and outside is where it must stay. You do not open the door to the voice, however gently it copies the cadence of someone you love, however perfectly it catches the small catch in your mother's laugh, because the copy is what it is fishing with; the real person, the tellers insist, is asleep beside you or far away in another estate and would never knock to be let into their own home, would simply use the keys, would call your phone, would scold you for keeping them waiting at the gate. Neither do you carry the tingkat inside, nor lift the lid to see what supper was sent, however the smell of it reaches you warm and familiar through the grille. To take it over the threshold is to accept the delivery, and a delivery accepted must, by the courtesy of these things, one day be returned in kind, carried by you to a door that opens on no corridor you know. You wait. By the first light, or the first real footstep of a neighbour leaving for the morning shift, it is gone, slip and all.

What lingers is the politeness of it, the patience, the way the voice never rises or pleads or hammers the way a living person locked out would, but simply repeats, content to wait you out across the locked gate for as long as the night allows, through the call to the first bird, through the hour the lift starts running again. Those who have lived through a visit say they did not sleep again for the warmth they felt through the metal when they finally, against all advice, crouched in the dark to touch the carrier. The detail that lands it: the tiffin is the old enamelled kind with a faded flower print, the sort your grandmother's generation sent hot meals to the sick and the newly delivered in, carried by hand across kampung and then across new estates, and threaded through its handle is a single rubber band, doubled, still holding, exactly the way your own family seals a packet of food to keep it from spilling on the way home from the coffeeshop downstairs.
note: This one lives in the high-floor corridors and the quieter HDB estates, Yishun and Sengkang in the newer tellings, the old Queenstown and Tiong Bahru walk-ups in the older ones. The night riders and the security guards swap it most, and it shows up on the parenting and ghost-story groups in the same breath, usually after someone posts about a food delivery they never placed turning up at midnight. The dread of it is that it borrows the most trusted sound there is, a known voice at your own door.

The variants split on the vessel: some say a tingkat, some a plastic bag of bak kut teh hung on the gate, some only the knock and the voice with nothing left behind at all. The ward is the same across all of them and it is hard precisely because it asks you to be unkind to a voice you cherish: do not answer, do not open, do not bring it in, and never call back through the door to ask who is there. Surely only a half-dream and a wrong-address delivery dressed up by a frightened mind, and yet I notice the people who tell it best are the ones who now check, every night, that everyone they love is accounted for before they sleep.
